questions.question: Explain the difference between a hierarchical directory structure and an acyclic graph directory structure. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: Hierarchical is tree-based with parent-child relationships
Acyclic allows sharing via links creating graph structure
Hierarchical: advantage is simple navigation, disadvantage is no sharing
Acyclic: advantage is file/directory sharing, disadvantage is complexity in traversal

questions.question: Describe the FCFS and SCAN disk scheduling algorithms. List one advantage and one disadvantage of SCAN over FCFS.
keyPoints: FCFS processes requests in arrival order
SCAN moves head in one direction servicing all, then reverses
SCAN advantage: reduced average seek time
SCAN disadvantage: requests at far ends may wait longer

questions.question: What is contiguous allocation for files? Explain why it suffers from external fragmentation.
keyPoints: Contiguous allocates consecutive disk blocks to files
External fragmentation: free blocks become scattered after file deletions/expansions
Cannot allocate large files when free space is fragmented into small pieces
Compaction needed to solve but is time-consuming

questions.question: Explain the purpose of the boot block and superblock in a file system. What information does the superblock typically contain?
keyPoints: Boot block contains bootstrap code to start OS
Superblock stores file system metadata
Contains: total disk blocks, free block count, free inode list, block size, file system type

questions.question: Describe the Bit Vector method for free space management. How does it help in efficient disk space allocation?
keyPoints: Bit vector represents each disk block as a bit (0=free, 1=allocated)
Provides O(1) time to find free blocks
Enables quick scanning for consecutive free blocks
Simple to implement and efficient for large disks

questions.question: What is the difference between linked allocation and indexed allocation for files? Mention one situation where indexed allocation is preferred.
keyPoints: Linked: each block points to next block, no external fragmentation but no random access
Indexed: separate index block contains pointers to all data blocks
Indexed preferred when random access is frequently needed
Indexed also better when file size is large and needs expansion

questions.question: Explain the concept of cylinder in disk geometry. How does cylinder concept help in reducing disk access time?
keyPoints: Cylinder is set of tracks at same radial position across all platter surfaces
All tracks in cylinder accessible without head movement
Requests for same cylinder can be serviced with minimal seek time
Reduces arm movement and rotational latency

questions.question: Explain the following disk scheduling algorithms with the help of a suitable example: FCFS, SSTF, and SCAN. Also mention their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: FCFS: Simple, fair but high seek time - example with queue
SSTF: Reduces seek time but may cause starvation - example shows closest first
SCAN: Elevator algorithm, better performance but unfair to edge requests
Advantages/disadvantages comparison for all three
Need to show how each algorithm handles same set of requests

questions.question: Discuss different directory implementation techniques. Compare linear list, hash table, and B-tree based implementations in terms of search time complexity and memory usage.
keyPoints: Linear list: simple O(n) search, low memory overhead
Hash table: O(1) average search but collision handling needed
B-tree: O(log n) search, balanced tree structure
Trade-offs: simplicity vs performance vs memory
Real-world examples of file systems using each method

questions.question: Describe the three main file allocation methods: contiguous, linked, and indexed. Compare them in terms of access time, external fragmentation, and ability to support random access.
keyPoints: Contiguous: sequential fast O(1), random access O(1), suffers external fragmentation
Linked: sequential access O(n), random access O(n), no external fragmentation
Indexed: random access O(1) via index block, no external fragmentation but index block overhead
Comparison table showing time complexity for sequential vs random access
Each method's suitability for different file access patterns

questions.question: Explain the structure of a disk and the various components of disk geometry. How does the physical organization of data affect disk performance?
keyPoints: Disk geometry: platters, tracks, sectors, cylinders
CHS addressing (Cylinder-Head-Sector) vs LBA (Logical Block Addressing)
Seek time, rotational latency, transfer time components
How data organization affects access time and throughput
Importance of locality and sequential access optimization
